title: Ecosystems & Habitats
objective: Students will explore ecosystems & habitats concepts including Changing Environments, Food Chains & Energy Transfer, Grouping Living Things and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Changing Environments? Let's find out together!
Recognise that environments can change and that this can sometimes pose dangers and challenges to living things
Let's practice together! Give at least two examples of environmental changes (natural and human-caused)
Can you give examples of how changes like pollution, building, or drought can make it harder for animals and plants to survive?

Great job! Today we learned about Changing Environments. Give at least two examples of environmental changes (natural and human-caused) Describe how a specific change affects the organisms in that environment
Have you ever thought about Food Chains & Energy Transfer? Let's find out together!
Construct and interpret food chains identifying producers, predators, and prey, and understand energy transfer between trophic levels
Time to try this out: Construct a food chain with at least four organisms, labelling producer, primary consumer, predator
Can you build a food chain for a woodland and point out which organisms are producers, which are predators, and which are prey?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Food Chains & Energy Transfer: Construct a food chain with at least four organisms, labelling producer, primary consumer, predator Define producer, predator, and prey with examples
Today we're going to learn about Grouping Living Things. Get ready for an adventure!
Recognise that living things can be grouped in a variety of ways based on observable features
Time to try this out: Group a set of organisms using at least two different criteria (e.g. by habitat, by body covering, by diet)
Can you sort a set of animals or plants into groups using their own criteria, like 'has wings' or 'lives in water'?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Grouping Living Things. Group a set of organisms using at least two different criteria (e.g. by habitat, by body covering, by diet) Explain the reasoning behind each grouping choice
Today we're going to learn about Classification Keys. Get ready for an adventure!
Explore and use classification keys to identify, group, and name living things in local and wider environments
Let me show you something cool. Follow a branching classification key to identify an unknown organism
Can you use a simple yes/no key chart to work out the name of an insect or plant they've found outside?
Great job! Today we learned about Classification Keys. Follow a branching classification key to identify an unknown organism Create a simple yes/no classification key for a set of 6-8 organisms
Let's discover the amazing world of Human impact on environments!

Use vocabulary for human impact on the environment — pollution, habitat destruction, deforestation, biodiversity, conservation, renewable energy, non-renewable energy, fossil fuel, carbon footprint, sustainability, endangered, extinct — and apply these when discussing environmental issues and human choices
Let's practice together! Distinguish between renewable and non-renewable energy sources using the correct terms and give examples of each
Can you explain what a carbon footprint is — not just that it's about pollution, but what it actually measures?

Great job! Today we learned about Human impact on environments. Distinguish between renewable and non-renewable energy sources using the correct terms and give examples of each Use 'biodiversity' and 'conservation' correctly in discussing why protecting habitats matters
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Animal Groups & Survival!
Construct an argument that some animals form groups that help members survive, such as herds, packs, or colonies
Let me show you something cool. Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools)
Can you explain why wolves hunt in packs or why fish swim in schools, and how being in a group helps them survive?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Animal Groups & Survival: Give at least two examples of animals that live in groups (e.g. wolves, ants, fish schools) Explain how group living provides survival advantages (protection, finding food, raising young)
Today we're going to learn about Ecology Vocabulary. Get ready for an adventure!

Use vocabulary for feeding relationships and ecological roles — producer, consumer, predator, prey, herbivore, carnivore, omnivore, decomposer, food chain, food web, nutrient cycle — and describe how energy and matter flow through ecosystems using these terms
Let me show you something cool. Construct a food chain correctly using producer, consumer, predator, and prey in the right positions
Can you draw a food chain from a plant to a top predator and label each animal as a producer, consumer, predator, or prey?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Ecology Vocabulary: Construct a food chain correctly using producer, consumer, predator, and prey in the right positions Distinguish between herbivore, carnivore, and omnivore with correct examples from a given ecosystem
Let's discover the amazing world of Reading Food Web Diagrams!

Read and interpret food web diagrams — identify producers, primary and secondary consumers, and decomposers; trace energy flow along food chains within the web; predict the effect of removing or adding a species
Time to try this out: Identify the producer, herbivore, and carnivore in a food web from a diagram
If you is shown a diagram with arrows connecting plants, insects, frogs, and hawks, can they trace which animals eat which and explain what would happen if the frogs disappeared?

Great job! Today we learned about Reading Food Web Diagrams. Identify the producer, herbivore, and carnivore in a food web from a diagram Trace two food chains through the same food web and identify shared species
